title: Kitchen of Love
author: Fablewick
lesson: Love doesn't always say its name. Sometimes it just hands you a mango and watches you eat.
summary: Ba doesn't hug. Ba doesn't say the words. Ba cuts fruit. Ba asks did you eat before how are you. The kid feels pestered. Then one day the kid sees Ba cutting fruit alone in the kitchen and understands everything.
coverIllustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='40' y='60' width='120' height='80' rx='8' fill='#D4925E' opacity='0.3'/><ellipse cx='100' cy='100' rx='25' ry='15' fill='#F0A830' opacity='0.6'/><path d='M90 95 Q100 80 110 95' stroke='#2A2118' stroke-width='1.5' fill='none'/><circle cx='70' cy='110' r='8' fill='#C45D3E' opacity='0.5'/><circle cx='130' cy='105' r='6' fill='#8FA68A' opacity='0.5'/></svg>

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
Ba lives in the kitchen.

Knife. Board. Fruit. That is Ba.

When you come to Ba's house, Ba does not say hello.

Ba says, "Did you eat?"

That is Ba's hello.

"Yes, Ba. I ate."

Ba nods. Ba disappears.

And then — fruit on the table. A mango, sliced thin, gold as the sun. A little fork beside it.

"Ba, I'm not hungry."

The fruit stays.

You eat it. Of course you eat it. It is sweet and cold and just right.

Ba watches from the doorway. Then Ba goes back.

Every visit. Every time. Fruit on the table.

One grey day, you come over quiet as a cat. Nobody knows you are coming.

Ba is in the kitchen. Ba is cutting a mango. Slow. Careful. The slices fall in a little gold row.

Nobody asked. Nobody is home.

Ba is cutting it just in case. Just in case somebody comes hungry.

Oh.

You walk in soft. You wrap your arms around Ba from behind.

"I love you, Ba."

Ba goes still. Ba's ears turn pink.

Ba does not say it back.

Ba cuts another mango.

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
Ba is always in the kitchen.

Other grandmothers sit in soft chairs. Other grandmothers watch television, or nap in the afternoon sun. Not Ba. Ba is in the kitchen, where the spice jars live and something is always warm on the stove.

When you come to Ba's house, Ba does not say hello. Ba does not say how are you.

Ba says, "Did you eat?"

Every visit. Every time.

"Yes, Ba. I ate before I came."

Ba nods, like that was the wrong answer, and disappears into the kitchen.

A few minutes later, fruit appears on the table. It just appears — the way rain appears, the way morning appears. A mango sliced into long gold crescents. Or a banana split down the middle. Or guava, cut open to show its pink heart, with a little fork resting on the plate.

"Ba, I'm not hungry."

Ba says nothing. The plate stays.

So you eat it. Of course you eat it. It is sweet and cold and exactly, perfectly ripe, because Ba checks the fruit every single morning and knows the very day it is ready.

Ba watches from the doorway. Just for a moment. Just long enough to see you take a bite. Then Ba goes back to the kitchen.

That is how it goes, every visit, your whole life. Sometimes you roll your eyes a little. Only to yourself.

Then one grey afternoon, you come over without calling. The door is unlocked. It always is. You step in quiet as a whisper.

Ba is in the kitchen. Ba does not know you are there.

Ba is standing at the counter with the small knife and the old wooden board, cutting a mango. Slowly. Carefully. Turning it, slicing it, laying the pieces in a neat gold row.

But nobody else is home. Nobody called. Nobody asked for anything.

Ba is cutting fruit for whoever might come. Just in case.

You stand in the doorway and watch Ba's careful hands, and something quiet clicks into place, like the last piece of a puzzle.

Did you eat. The plate on the table. The fruit, always ready.

That was the saying of it. All along, that was the saying of it.

You cross the kitchen and wrap your arms around Ba from behind.

"I love you, Ba."

Ba goes very still.

Ba's ears turn pink.

Ba doesn't say it back. Ba never has.

Ba pats your hand — once, twice. Then Ba picks up the knife.

And Ba cuts another mango.

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
Ba is always in the kitchen.

You have known this your whole life. Other grandmothers sit in the garden, maybe, or doze in the good chair by the window, or fold laundry in front of the evening news. Not Ba. Ba is in the kitchen. The kitchen is where Ba lives. The rest of the house is just rooms.

The kitchen has a smell that belongs to nowhere else in the world: cumin from a jar older than you are, warm oil, and the particular sweetness of fruit at the exact right moment of ripe. You have known that smell since you were small enough to sit up on the counter and watch Ba's hands work.

When you come to Ba's house, Ba hears you. Ba always hears, even when you tiptoe. And the first thing Ba says is not hello, and not how are you, and not it is so good to see you.

The first thing Ba says, every time, is: "Did you eat?"

"Yes, Ba. I ate before I came."

Ba makes a small sound, somewhere between a hm and a we'll see, and turns back to the cutting board.

A few minutes later, a plate appears on the table. It arrives the way weather arrives: no announcement, no ceremony. A mango sliced into long gold crescents, fanned out just so. Or a banana split neatly down the middle. Or guava when it is in season, cut open to show the pink inside. Or papaya, if Ba found a good one at the market. And Ba goes to the market every few days and takes it very seriously.

"Ba, I said I'm not hungry."

Ba does not look up. "Eat a little."

So you eat. And you are, it turns out, a little hungry. The mango is perfect, cold and sweet, not one stringy bite, because Ba checks the fruit every morning, pressing each one gently with a thumb, moving it from counter to refrigerator at exactly the right moment. Never once in your whole life has Ba handed you a piece of fruit that was not ready.

Ba watches from the doorway while you eat. Just for a moment. Just long enough. Then Ba goes back to the kitchen.

That is every visit. That has been every visit your whole life.

When you were little, you never thought about it. As you got older, it started to itch. You were not hungry. You had not asked. The fruit appeared anyway, and Ba stood in the doorway watching, and the whole thing felt a bit like being seven years old forever.

"I already ate, Ba."

"Did you eat enough?"

There was no right answer to that one. In Ba's arithmetic, there is no such number as enough.

Then comes a grey afternoon in November: the sky low, the light the color of old paper. You go over without calling. You don't always call. The door is never locked. You let yourself in, and the house is quiet, which only means Ba is in the kitchen being quiet.

You set your bag down and walk toward the kitchen — and stop in the doorway.

Ba is standing at the counter with the small sharp knife and the wooden cutting board that has lived in this kitchen since before you were born, worn to a shallow curve in the middle from years and years of use. In Ba's hands, a mango.

Ba is cutting it the way Ba always does: feeling for the flat of the seed, turning the fruit, drawing the knife along the curve so each slice comes away clean. The gold pieces fall into a neat row, and Ba arranges them without even thinking, the way Ba has arranged them a thousand times.

But nobody else is home. Nobody called ahead. Nobody asked for anything.

Ba doesn't know you are there.

Ba is just cutting a mango. Alone, in the grey light, for whoever might come. Just in case.

You stand in the doorway and watch. The kitchen smells the way it always smells. Ba's hands are slow and sure. Ba has cut fruit in this kitchen for fifty years.

And something shifts in you — not words, exactly. More like a picture coming into focus. A picture you had been standing too close to your whole life, and now, one step back, you can finally see what it is.

Did you eat.

Eat a little.

The plate on the table, asked for or not.

Ba pressing each mango every morning, learning the exact day of ripe.

Ba, alone, on a grey afternoon, cutting fruit just in case.

It was always the same sentence. Said in the only language Ba speaks.

You cross the kitchen. Ba hears you and starts to turn, surprised — but you get there first. You wrap your arms around Ba from behind, your cheek against Ba's shoulder.

"I love you, Ba."

Ba goes completely still.

Then you feel it: the warmth of Ba's hand coming to rest over yours. A pat. Another. The thing Ba does when there are no words.

Ba's ears have gone pink.

Ba doesn't say it back. Ba has never said it. It is not Ba's language.

After a moment, Ba straightens up gently, and you let go, and Ba turns back to the cutting board.

Ba cuts another mango. Ba lays the slices on a plate. Ba sets the plate on the table without looking at you.

"Eat," Ba says. "You look thin."

You sit down. You eat. It is perfect.

And Ba stands in the doorway and watches you eat, all the way to the last gold slice.
